coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots from tea enhance z-type BiOBr/C3N4 heterojunctions for RhB degradation: Catalytic effect, mechanisms, and intermediates

Zhang, Xinyu

coredata.dc:description: The environmental crisis is an important factor that restricts human development. Biomass-derived carbon is an excellent material for the treatment of organic pollutants; however, realization of the energy band regulation of semiconductor photocatalysts remains challenging. Herein, we used green tea as a raw material to obtain different-colored carbon dots and hydrothermal carbon and introduced the C3N4/BiOBr Z-scheme heterostructure for enhancing electron transfer and band regulation. The experimental results show that the material exhibits good photocatalytic performance. 98.48% of rhodamine B (RhB) was degraded within 15 min by 20 wt% red light emitting carbon quantum dot doped C3N4/BiOBr, indicating that its performance is 4.07 times that of BiOBr. The electron spin resonance indicates that the composite material generates ·OH and ·O2 − radicals and the reaction mechanisms of the catalyst and the intermediate from the RhB degradation. This study provides a new strategy for the development of biomass-derived carbon photocatalysis.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots from tea enhance z-type BiOBr/C3N4 heterojunctions for RhB degradation: Catalytic effect, mechanisms, and intermediates

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The environmental crisis is an important factor that restricts human development. Biomass-derived carbon is an excellent material for the treatment of organic pollutants; however, realization of the energy band regulation of semiconductor photocatalysts remains challenging. Herein, we used green tea as a raw material to obtain different-colored carbon dots and hydrothermal carbon and introduced the C3N4/BiOBr Z-scheme heterostructure for enhancing electron transfer and band regulation. The experimental results show that the material exhibits good photocatalytic performance. 98.48% of rhodamine B (RhB) was degraded within 15 min by 20 wt% red light emitting carbon quantum dot doped C3N4/BiOBr, indicating that its performance is 4.07 times that of BiOBr. The electron spin resonance indicates that the composite material generates ·OH and ·O2 − radicals and the reaction mechanisms of the catalyst and the intermediate from the RhB degradation. This study provides a new strategy for the development of biomass-derived carbon photocatalysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental pollution is a crucial issue that is affecting a growing society [1]. The removal of organic pollutants, including volatile organic compounds, dyes, and antibiotics, is challenging owing to their wide variety, extensive production, and wide distribution [2]. Traditional methods, such as adsorption [3], thermal catalysis [4], and ozonation [5], cannot completely remove these pollutants and the operation is cumbersome. Semiconductor heterojunction photocatalysis has attracted widespread attention due to its simple and efficient degradation [6]. However, the accumulation of organic pollutants on catalyst surfaces, the high electron-hole recombination rate during catalysis, and the influence of the energy band structure tend to render the catalytic efficiency of these methods poor [7–8]. Thus, modifying heterojunction photocatalytic materials to enhance their efficiency as catalysts is of increasing importance.

Graphitic phase carbon nitride (C3N4) is a 2D nonmetallic semiconductor material that, compared with other semiconductor photocatalysts, has a moderate energy band structure (2.7–2.8 eV), a good visible light absorption range, and excellent physicochemical properties. Thus, C3N4 shows potential application in the field of photocatalysis [9–10]. However, the small specific area and high recombination rate of the photogenerated electrons and holes in C3N4 limit its application [11].To solve these problems, ultrasonic exfoliation can be used to obtain ultrathin g-C3N4 nanosheets that can improve the separation of photogenerated carriers or form heterojunction structures with other semiconductors, improving the energy band structure of the material [12]. Currently, C3N4/CeO2 [13], C3N4/NiCo2O4 [14], and C3N4/CoSe2 [15] have been used to produce hydrogen via organic matter degradation, carbon dioxide reduction, and water decomposition.

Recently, much attention has been focused on bismuth oxyhalides (BiOX, X = Cl, Br, and I) owing to their unique tetragonal layered structures and indirect transition band gap. They are conducive to the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes by electrostatic layers and thus have attracted extensive attention for the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollution in water [16–17]. Fangfang Duo [18] used D-Glucose and ethylenediamine as carbon sources to obtain N-CQDs, which were then mixed with BiOBr precursors to successfully synthesize CQDs/BiOBr. The upconversion of the CQDs refreshed the oxygen vacancies on the BiOBr, maintaining its photocatalytic stability, and the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of the resulting catalyst for rhodamine B (RhB) and bisphenol A (BPA) reached more than 90% within 20 min.

Biomass-derived carbon materials, such as activated carbon, water and heat carbon, carbon quantum dots, and active carbon fibers, are prepared using biomass as a raw material. Carbon quantum dots have excellent upconversion luminescence and control the energy band structure of a catalyst. Yang Hao [19] used cellulose as a source to prepare carbon dots and achieved a removal rate of 83% for phenol (10 mg/L−1) using the synthesized g-C3N4/C-140, which is 3 times that of the original g-C3N4. However, the hydrothermal residue was not utilized, which is disadvantageous. Xiaoyun Xi [20] prepared carbon quantum dots using photodeposition to synthesize Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs photocatalyst from corncob biomass and utilized the hydrothermal residue for the synthesis of Fe3O4/BiOBr/HC photocatalyst. The experimental results showed a removal rate of greater than 95% for carbamazepine using both materials under visible light. Generally, carbon quantum dots produced directly via the hydrothermal synthesis of biomass mainly emit blue light; however, research into red light–emitting carbon dots is currently lacking.

Herein, we used green tea as a raw material to innovatively prepare red/blue carbon quantum dots, which were then combined with C3N4/BiOBr heterogeneous knot material. The effective separation of the energy band structure within the heterolytic materials by the carbon dots led to photochemical electron-vacancy separation. The produced materials were used in the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants with RhB. This study provides a comprehensive utilization direction for waste biomass resources and includes an in-depth discussion of the synergistic effect of multicolor light–emitting carbon dots on the heterogeneous optical catalytic system and optical degradation reaction mechanism. This work provides new strategies for the application of biomass carbon–based catalytic materials in the environment and energy.

Tie Guanyin green tea was obtained from a market in Hohhot, the Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region, China, and crushed. Anhydrous ethanol (Tianjin Kemao Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China), methanol, silver nitrate (Tianjin FengChuan Chemical Reagent Technology Co., Ltd., China), melamine (Mreda technology Inc., USA), thiourea (Tianjing FuChen Chemical Reagent Technology Co., Ltd., China), sodium hydroxide, potassium hydroxide (Tianjing Zhiyuan chemical reagent Technology Co., Ltd., China), ethylenediamine (Tianjin Beilian Refine Chemical Development Co., Ltd., China), potassium bromide, hydrochloric acid (Chinese Medicine Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), ammonium oxalate, isopropyl alcohol, p-benzoquinone, bismuth nitrate 99.9% (Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.) were all used in this study. Unless specifically mentioned, chemicals were used as received.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of red CQD (RCD) from tea
ce:section.ce:para: Red carbon quantum dots were prepared via the hydrothermal method using anhydrous ethanol as a solvent and the tea solution that was obtained following Soxhlet extraction. Generally, 10.00 g of green tea powder was weighed and the Soxhlet was extracted in 300 mL of ethanol solution at 90 °C for 18 h. The upper layer of the tea residue was removed for further use and the remaining solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm. Subsequently, 60 mL of supernatant was placed in a 100 mL Teflon kettle and reacted at 160 °C for 4 h. The solution was then cooled, centrifuged at 10000 rpm, and filtered through a 0.22-μm filter membrane. The solution was then separated and freeze-dried to obtain red carbon dots (RCD).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of blue CQD from tea
ce:section.ce:para: Blue carbon quantum dots were prepared hydrothermally using the residual tea obtained in the previous step. Briefly, 3.00 g of light green tea residue, 0.5-mL ethylenediamine, and 60-mL distilled water were placed in a 100-mL beaker and stirred for 30 min before transferring the mixed solution to a Teflon reactor, where it was reacted at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the hydrothermal product was extracted and washed with water and an ethanol solution until the filtrate was colorless. The upper residue was retained for further use, while the lower solution was concentrated by spin evaporation and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was then filtered using a 0.22 aqueous filter head and dialyzed in a 1000–1500 D dialysis bag for 2 d. The resulting solution was then spin-concentrated and freeze-dried to obtain blue carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of hydrothermal carbon from tea
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrothermal carbon (HTC) was prepared by high-temperature KOH activation using the residue from the previous step. Residue was added to potassium hydroxide (g:g = 1:1, 1:2, and 1:3) in a beaker, 30 mL of distilled water was added, and the results were stirred for 30 min before drying in a vacuum at 105 °C. The products were then calcined in a tube furnace at 800 °C for 2 h under a N2 atmosphere at a heating rate of 5 °C/min. After cooling, the products were removed and washed with 0.5 M HCl and distilled water until neutral, dried at 105 °C, and named HTC-1, HCT-2, and HCT-3.

C3N4 was prepared via thermal polycondensation. Briefly, melamine and thiourea (g:g = 5:5) were added to 40 mL of methanol solution and stirred for 20 min before drying at 60 °C. The product was then placed in an alumina crucible covered with aluminum foil paper and reacted at 550 °C for 4 h at a heating rate of 2.5 °C/min before cooling. The product was removed and ground until it was free of particles; the obtained sample was named SCN.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the CQDs/CN/BiOBr composite photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs/CN/BiOBr were synthesized at room temperature. First, 0.136 g of SCN, 1.58 g of Bi(NO3)3, and different amounts of CQDs were placed in a 40 mL of ethylene glycol solution and subjected to ultrasound for 3 h. The obtained solution was named Solution A. Meanwhile, 0.476 g of KBr and 0.16 g of NaOH were dissolved in distilled water and subjected to ultrasound for 3 h, and the solution was added gradually to Solution A, which was then stirred for 30 min and left for 4 h. The product was then centrifuged multiple times with water and ethanol at 10,000 rpm and dried at 105 °C. Depending on the mass content of CQDs in the sample, products were named R1-4 and B1-2 (R1: 5 wt%, R2: 10 wt%, R3: 15 wt%, R4: 20 wt%, R5: 25 wt%, B1: 5 wt%, and B2: 10 wt%). The preparation process is shown in Scheme 1 .

A total of 0.01 g of catalysts and RhB solution (30 mg/L, 100 mL) were placed in photocatalytic quartz tubes and allowed to react in the dark until reaching adsorption/desorption equilibrium for 30 min before photodegradation. The light source (mercury lamp: 500 W and xenon lamp: 300 W) was then activated and samples were collected at certain time intervals. A clear supernatant was also obtained by centrifuging at 12,000 rpm and the absorbance was measured in an ultraviolet (UV)–visible (Vis) spectrophotometer (UV-8000S, Shanghai, China). The experiments were repeated and the average value was used as the final result. Because of the important role played by reactive radicals in the photocatalytic reaction process, 2 mmol silver nitrate (AgNO3), isopropyl alcohol (IPA), ammonium oxalate (AO), and 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ) were used as electron (e−), hydroxyl radicals (·OH), photogenerated vacancies (h + ), and superoxide radicals (·O2 −) scavengers, respectively.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Phenom Pro; Funa Scientific Instruments (Shanghai) Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) was performed at an accelerating voltage of 10 kV. A small number of samples were placed on conductive adhesive and gold sprayed (small ion sputtering instrument, Beijing Zhongke Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China) for morphology observation. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed using a FEI Tecnai S-200-kV twin field emission high-resolution transmission electron microscope (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Massachusetts, USA). Samples for TEM were diluted with anhydrous ethanol and dried on copper mesh for testing. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was conducted on the Empyrean Alpha-1, Shanghai SIBG Instrument System Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China, using Cu Ka radiation at a generator voltage of 40 kV and a current of 40 mA. FTIR spectra were obtained using a Perkin Elmer 65 (Perkin Elmer Instruments Co., Ltd., Waltham, Massachusetts, USA) from 4000 cm−1 to 400 cm−1. The cumulative number of scans was 32 and the resolution was 1 cm−1. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) for all materials was determined using the Micromeritics ASAP 2460 static nitrogen adsorption apparatus. Samples were vacuum-pretreated at 105 °C for 3 h to remove water and air and the specific surface area of the catalyzer was determined in a liquid nitrogen environment. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded using a UV spectrophotometer (TU1950-1950, Beijing General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China) over a range of 200–800 nm. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured using a F98 spectrofluorophotometer (Shanghai Prism Technology Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China) equipped with a 150 W Xe lamp. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi with a single Al X-ray source. The charging effect was corrected by adjusting the C1 binding energy to 284.6 eV. Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP–MS) was conducted on the Agilent 5110 instrument. Electron spin resonance (ESR) was performed on a Bruker EMXplus-6/1. Briefly, a 1-mg/mL sample was configured using methanol as a solvent and ultrasonic dispersion was performed for 5 min before 30 μL of DMPO was added to 30 μL of solvent (200-mM methanol). The resulting solution was mixed well and a capillary tube was used to absorb the required amount of mixture, which was placed in the EPR sample cavity for ·OH/O2 · radical testing. A 300-W Xe lamp was used as the light source. After illumination for 0, 5, and 10 min, photoelectrochemical testing was conducted using a CHI760E, Shanghai. The open circuit potential, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), photocurrent response (PCR), and Mott–Schottky plot (M − S) were employed under the standard three-electrode system. A saturated calomel electrode and Ag/AgCl plate were utilized as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively. Meanwhile, 100 mg catalyst, 2 mL anhydrous ethanol and 20 μL Nafion*D 521 dispersion were mixed and ground homogeneously, the mixture was transferred to a FTO glass and dried by infrared light irradiation for 20 min as a working electrode. A 300-W Xe lamp was selected as a light source and all photoelectrochemical tests were studied at room temperature in a Na2SO4 solution (0.5 M, pH 7). The EIS test was performed over a frequency range of 300 kHz to 0.1 Hz with a 5 mV AC amplitude.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of ethanol extract components in tea
ce:section.ce:para: The alcohol-soluble active components of tea are essential for the synthesis of red light–emitting carbon quantum dots. Fig.S1a shows a photograph of the tea leaves obtained during the Soxhlet extraction process. Fig.S1b shows the fluorescence plot for the ethanol extracted from tea leaves at different concentrations. The extracted solution emitted a bright red light under the UV lamp at 365 nm, mainly because of the presence of chlorophyll [21]. The ethanol extract was first analyzed by UV–Vis spectroscopy to obtain the possible components; both chlorophyll (400–700 nm) and tea polyphenols (200–300 nm) were observed, as seen in Fig.S1c. The ethanol extract was separated on a silica gel column (200–300 mesh) (Fig. S1d–e), and UV–Vis spectroscopy was applied to the solutions at different stages of separation. As shown in Fig. 1 a–c, chlorophyll a, β-carotene, and an unknown component, which is presumed to be chlorophyll b, xanthophyll, or α-carotene, were successfully isolated from the ethanol extract. Ultra performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry analysis(UPLC–MS) was used to gain a deeper understanding of the components in the ethanol extract (Fig.S2). The low mass spectral peaks at m/z = 892.52, 907.82, 569.77, and 537.75 indicate the presence of chlorophyll a, chlorophyll b, xanthophyll, and α-carotene, respectively. Meanwhile, the strong peaks at m/z = 195.37, m/z = 458.54, and m/z = 443.39 in the mass spectra correspond to 1,3,7-trimethylxanthine, tea polyphenol, and epicatechin gallate, respectively, indicating that the ethanolic extracts contain a high level of the above substances (Fig. 1d). In addition, the relatively weak mass spectra peaks at m/z = 291.47, m/z = 307.34, and m/z = 159.47 indicate that catechin, gallocatechin, and L-theanine were still present in the solution [22]. The large number of benzene rings with polyphenolic structures provides a suitable chemical environment for the synthesis of red light–emitting carbon quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of RCD and BCD
ce:section.ce:para: RCD and BCD are synthesized via different preparation processes. Both RCD and BCD solutions appear brown under room light, and emit red and blue fluorescence, respectively, under ultraviolet light (Fig. 2 a). The absorption spectra in Fig. 2b disclose the reason for this; BCD exhibits weak absorption peaks at 200–500 nm, while RCD exhibits a broad absorption band covering UV and visible regions at 200–700 nm. These findings are similar to the results obtained by Jun Liu et al [23] . The photoluminescence mechanism of carbon dots is explained in terms of carbon nucleus, molecular, and surface states [24]. Compared with BCD, the π–π* transition of RCD in the range of 225–300 nm is generally sourced in the sp2 conjugated domain of the carbon nucleus state. The higher number of conjugated domains in RCD results in red fluorescence, with the molecular states of RCD at 500–650 nm mainly derived from macromolecular structures on the surface [25]. The solubility difference between RCD and BCD was obtained using FTIR (Fig. 2c). Similar functional groups were observed in the different samples, with the peaks at 3050–3600 cm−1 associated with O − H and N − H stretching vibrations [26], peaks at 1669–1738 cm−1 attributed to C = O and C–N stretching vibrations, and strong peaks that are associated with C = C (1620 cm−1) and C–C stretching vibrations (1452 cm−1) in the aromatic ring skeleton of RCD indicating numerous sp2 structures and a C–H bending vibration. The C–N bonds peak at 1300–1400 cm−1 and strong BCD absorption peaks at 1050 cm−1 (C–O–C stretching vibration) indicate favorable water solubility. Conversely, the appearance of a –CH2 swaying vibration at 720 cm−1 indicates that RCD has a certain degree of hydrophobicity. Furthermore, the XRD spectrum for CQDs in Fig.S3a shows a diffraction peak near 2θ = 20°, which may be related to the (002) crystal plane of the graphite [27].

Fluorescence spectroscopy is a means of directly obtaining the spectral performance of carbon quantum dots. Based on the fluorescence excitation spectrum (Fig.S3b), BCD excitation is at 350–450 nm, while RCD excitation occurs at 300–700 nm, with the maximum excitation wavelengths of 407 and 665 nm, respectively, indicating typical two-photon emission [28]. This can also be seen in the 2D fluorescence spectrum in Fig.S3c–d. The fluorescence emission spectrum of the carbon dots (Fig. 2d) indicates a fluorescence emission range of 420–700 nm for BCD, with the maximum emission peak at 480 nm. In contrast to BCD, RCD exhibits approximate fluorescence emission curves under 407 and 665 nm excitation, with a maximum absorption peak at around 675 nm and a shoulder peak at 720 nm, demonstrating a local emissive-like character that is associated with the vibrational fine structure observed in the PL spectra. This is consistent with the results obtained by Wen-Cheng Chen et al. [29] . Furthermore, comparison of the quantum yield of quinine sulfate indicates fluorescence quantum yields of 8.2% and 4.4% for RCD and BCD, respectively. The excellent photoluminescence performance of the carbon dots is assumed to promote photocatalysis.

TEM was used to obtain the overall morphology of the CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2(e, f), compared with the BCD’s spherical structure, the sheet-shaped RCD may bind more easily to C3N4, improving the overall photocatalytic performance of the material. Fig.S4 shows the lattice fringes of RCD and BCD, with 0.33 and 0.21 nm referring to the (002) and (100) crystal planes in the graphite crystals, respectively.

To intuitively understand the hydrophobicity of the two types of carbon quantum dots, water contact angle tests were conducted on RCD and BCD, with the experimental results given in Fig.S5. The water contact angle of 127° for RCD indicates good hydrophobicity. However, the water contact angle of 0° for BCD indicates good water solubility. The hydrophobicity of RCD is associated with functional groups that remain following the dehydration and condensation of tea polyphenols, chlorophyll, and other molecules, which also provide corresponding chemical active sites that allow better combination with the catalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology, structure, and elemental analysis of CQDs/CN/BiOBr
ce:section.ce:para: Surface morphology, element distribution, and structural characteristics are intuitive means of understanding a material. Fig.S6 is the catalyst; SCN and BiOBr are yellow and white in color, and the carbon point loadings R4 and B1 are brown-green and gray in color, respectively. SEM images of SCN, BiOBr, and CN/BiOBr are shown in Fig.S7, in which SCN (Fig.S7a) exhibits obvious layered stacking and BiOBr (Fig.S7b) exhibits a macroscopic agglomeration state. Notably, CN/BiOBr (Fig.S7c) is dispersed on the surface of CN and ultrasound causes the SCN to peel off to a certain extent, improving the loading. No considerable change was observed in the SEM images of R4 (Fig. 3 a) and B1 (Fig. 3d) as compared to CN/BiOBr after CQD loading. However, carbon dots are obviously dispersed on the surface of CN in the TEM image. The HRTEM image (Fig. 3b–f) displays clear lattice fringes with interplanar spacings of 0.25, 0.82, and 0.21 nm, which can be indexed to the (100) plane of SCN, the (001) plane of BiOBr, and the (100) crystallographic plane of carbon, respectively [30]. BiOBr occurs in long, rod-shaped accumulations, which are conducive to the formation of pores and improve light utilization. Carbon dots form connecting sites between the CN and BiOBr, with the planar structure of the RCD more conducive to contact. The results of the mapping that was used to analyze the surface element distribution of the sample (Fig.S8 and Fig. 3g–n) indicated that compared to B1, considerably lower numbers of C and N elements were exposed in R4 and the surface contained abundant Bi. This verified that the BiOBr was wrapped tightly on the surface of the CN in R4, indicating that RCD plays a considerable role in connecting the BiOBr to the CN.

To obtain a more accurate understanding of the elemental content of the material, EDX spectroscopy and ICP–MS were used to classify the catalyst elements. As depicted in Fig.S9 and Table S1, the surface C (1.64 wt%) and S (0.01 wt%) of R4 are significantly reduced compared to the C (7.49 wt%) and S (0.41 wt%) in B1. However, the S and Bi content of the catalysts in ICP–MS are similar, indicating that the lipid-soluble RCD binds more effectively, with carbon dot deposition observed on the catalyst. The chlorophyll in the tea means that there may be trace amounts of the element Mg in the carbon quantum dots, as confirmed by the results of mapping, EDX, and ICP–MS analysis, which indicated surface Mg element contents of 0.12 wt% and 0.11 wt% in R4 and B1, respectively, accounting for 0.0046 wt% of the total catalyst mass. Different from the traditional carbon dots that use citric acid as a source material, trace amounts of Mg may enhance the absorption of light and electron transfer in the catalyst.

The specific surface area of a catalyst is a key factor that affects its photocatalytic performance. In general, a rich, porous structure is conducive to the exposure of the active site. The pore structure of the catalyst was therefore studied using N2 adsorption–desorption curves (Fig. 3o). All samples presented type III isotherms with H3 hysteresis loops, indicating that the pores were mainly in the form of flat slits. The specific surfaces of CN/BiOBr, R4, and B1 were 33.41 m2 g−1, 89.26 m2 g−1, and 40.58 m2 g−1, respectively, with the rich specific surface of R4 coming from the interlacing of the RCD lamellar structure between BiOBr and CN. R4 has a large number of active sites, leading to ultrahigh adsorption and catalytic effects. Fig.S10 and Table S3, which show the pore-size distribution of the catalyst, indicate that all the studied materials have good mesoporous structures. However, the pore-size distribution characteristics of the various catalysts differ, with pore-size distributions of 3–50 nm, 3–28 nm, and 3–48 nm for CN/BiOBr, R4, and B1, respectively. In particular, the pores of R4 are mainly concentrated at around 6.68 nm. Compared to the average pore size of CN/BiOBr (13.3705 nm) and B1 (12.5027 nm), R4 has a more concentrated average pore size of 7.9498 nm, indicating its potential application in photocatalysis.

To further investigate the superior photocatalytic performance of CQDs/BiOBr/CN, XPS was used to evaluate the elemental valence and composition of the heterojunction photocatalysts. Full and high-resolution XPS spectra are shown in Fig.S11 and Fig. 4 , respectively. The binding energy of R4 is generally low, improving the coupling between the heterostructures. The coupling effect can also be seen from the fact that the peak area of R4 is higher than that of B1. According to the C 1 s XPS analysis of R4 and B1 (Fig. 4a), the peaks at 288.6 eV, 285.3 eV, and 284.7 eV indicate the presence of O–C = O/N–C = N, C–O/C–N, and C–H/C–C bonds, respectively, in the CQDs and carbon nitride. C–OH and C = O bonds that are provided by carbon quantum dots are located near 286.3 and 286.0 eV, respectively [31]. Bi 4f (Fig. 4b), which is derived from BiOBr, shows typical bimodal peaks at 164.8 eV (Bi 2f5/2) and 159.5 eV (Bi 2f7/2), which are assigned to the chemical state Bi3+ [32]. In the XPS spectrum for S 2P (Fig. 4c), S–O and S–C bonds peak around 164.8 and 595.5 eV, respectively [33]. The O1S spectrum (Fig. 4d) of the composite material indicates the presence of hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface of the carbon quantum dots and Bi-O bonds in the BiOBr [34]. The peaks at 402.2, 401.4, and 399.3 eV are associated with the C–N = C skeleton structure of the aromatic ring, hybridized sp3 in the (N–C3) aromatic ring plane, and C–NHx, respectively (Fig. 4e). Meanwhile, the XPS valence band spectrum is an effective means of characterizing the valence band of semiconductor materials. Fig. 4f shows the XPS valence band spectrum of the catalyst. Valence band positions at 1.64 and 1.82 eV were observed in R4 and B1, respectively. These results indicate that the addition of carbon dots can change the chemical properties of materials to a certain extent.

The phase structure and crystallinity of CN, BiOBr, CN/BiOBr, R4, and B1 were studied using XRD, as shown in Fig. 5 a, The planar structure filling motifs and interlayer stacking of CN aromatic segments peak at 13.0° and 27.3°, respectively [35]. The crystal structure of BiOBr conforms to the tetragonal phase (JCPDS No. 09–0393) [36], with typical diffraction peaks at 10.8°, 25.2°, 32.3°, 39.4°, 46.4°, and 51.7° corresponding to (001), (101), (110), (112), (200), and (212) planes, respectively. Nevertheless, no diffraction peaks were observed for CQDs or CN in the XRD patterns of R4 and B1, which might be due to low content. Notably, because RCD combines more stably with the catalyst, the diffraction peak intensity was lower in R4 than B1, illustrating that using photoinduction for R4 synthesis results in high catalytic activity potential.

FTIR was used to obtain the surface functional group composition of the catalysts. Fig. 5b shows the FTIR spectra obtained from the catalysts; the peak at 510 cm−1 is a Bi–O stretching vibration bond from BiOBr [37]. Meanwhile, the three absorption peaks that were observed at 808 cm−1, 1140–1640 cm−1, and 3000–3600 cm−1 for CN correspond to the bending vibration of the triazine ring structure, the stretching vibration peak of the aromatic carbon–nitrogen heterocyclic compounds, and the N–H stretching vibration, respectively [38]. After loading the CQDs, the composite material showed absorption peaks for CN and BiOBr, indicating the successful synthesis of the catalyst.

The light absorption performance of a catalyst is an important indicator of its photocatalytic performance. The UV–visible diffuse reflectance absorption spectrum (DRS) of the photocatalyst is shown in Fig. 5c. The results indicate that SCN has a strong adsorption band below 463 nm; BiOBr exhibits a lower visible light absorption with an absorption edge at 416 nm when combined with CN; the catalyst has a wider visible light absorption. Notably, R4 has stronger visible light absorption performance and a wider adsorption range compared to the other catalysts. The Motschottky curve is a method of obtaining the flat band potential (Efb) of a material. In n-type semiconductors, the Efb is approximately equal to the conduction band position of the material. Fig.S12(a–e) show Motschottky curves of CN, BiOBr, CN/BiOBr, R4, and B1. The formula E(NHE) = E(Ag/AgCl) + 0.197 was used to convert the Motschottky curve into the standard hydrogen electrode potential (NHE). The conduction bands (CBs) of − 0.123, −0.523, −0.193, −0.333, and − 0.533 eV that were obtained for CN, BiOBr, and CN/BiOBr, R4, and B1, respectively, are estimated from the horizontal intercepts as 1/C2 equals zero. Meanwhile, the theoretical band positions of CN, BiOBr, and CN/BiOBr were obtained (Fig. 5d) by combining the Tauc plots (Fig.S12f) [39], and the change in the band structure confirms the formation of heterojunctions. In fact, combined with the XPS valence band spectra, the CBs in R4 and B1 were calculated to be − 0.53 and − 0.77 eV (Fig. 4(e-f)), with the changes in bandgap and energy band positions indicating that the introduction of CQDS greatly improves light utilization.

Fluorescence emission spectroscopy is an effective method by which the efficiency of charge recombination can be analyzed. A decrease in the fluorescence intensity indicates a lower efficiency for photogenerated electron hole recombination, which is more conducive to catalytic reactions [40]. Furthermore, Fig. 5g indicates that the fluorescence intensity of R4 and B1 is lower than that of the other catalysts, indicating limited recombination of the photogenerated charge carriers, which in turn promotes catalysis. The dissection of the transient photocurrent analysis in Fig. 5h indicates that the magnitude of the photocurrent reflects the separation efficiency of hole pairs, with R4 and B1 exhibiting maximum photocurrent values [41]. EIS is an important means of studying the migration rate of photogenerated charge carriers. As shown in Fig. 5i, the arc radius of the R4 Nyquist diagram was considerably smaller than those of CN and BiOBr, indicating that the introduction of carbon dots improves the dissociation and charge transfer characteristics of a charge carrier by rendering the generation of electrons and holes easier during photocatalytic degradation. Thus, PL spectroscopy, photoelectrochemical testing, and EIS show that the introduction of CQDs has a desirable regulatory effect on the separation efficiency and carrier migration rate of the catalysts.

To evaluate the photocatalytic activity of the prepared materials for water purification, RhB was photodegraded under a mercury lamp. Fig.S13(a–d) show photocatalytic degradation curves for CN/BiOBr. When loaded with different concentrations of RCD and BCD, R4 and B1 show the best photocatalytic performance with reaction rate constants of − 0.2653 and − 0.1068 min−1, respectively. This is because loading a few carbon points limits the catalytic ability, whereas excessive carbon points can lead to self-aggregation, which is not conducive to light or electron transfer. Because sheet RCD has more conjugated structures and stronger light absorption ability compared to BCD, the catalytic rate of R4 is much higher than that of B1. Fig. 6 a shows the poor degradation of RhB by pure SCN. For pristine BiOBr and CN/BiOBr, only approximately 75% and 70% of the RhB was degraded after 20 min of UV irradiation, respectively. Notably, regulating the band structure with carbon dots led to a considerable enhancement in the catalytic effect of RhB. Notably, the catalytic rate of R4 was 4.07 and 3.15 times that of BiOBr and CN/BiOBr, respectively (Fig. 6b).

Reaction conditions are known to be important factors that constrain catalytic reactions. Generally, the amount of catalyst used is related to adsorption in dark reactions and the absorption of light in the photocatalytic process, as indicated in Fig. 6c. Increasing the catalyst dosage enhanced the adsorption performance of R4 on RhB, rendering the adsorption effect much greater than the catalytic performance, which is not conducive to reasonable disclosure of the process. At a catalyst dosage of 10 mg, it is obvious that the material has reached adsorption equilibrium. Fig. 6d shows the degradation curves for different RhB concentrations. As the RhB concentration increases, the dark reaction is more likely to reach adsorption equilibrium. However, as the degradation time required for photocatalysis increases, the lack of RhB results in a much greater adsorption effect as compared to catalysis. Therefore, a RhB concentration of 30 mg/L was selected as the optimal choice. The initial pH of the pollutant is an essential variable that requires consideration when striving to enhance the efficiency of a system. From Fig. 6e, it can be seen that at a pH of between 3 and 9, the catalyst has desirable stability with small changes. On the one hand, low or high pH can promote the adsorption of RhB on the catalyst surface, while on the other, it can effectively inhibit the recombination of electrons and holes, thereby promoting the catalytic reaction. Notably, at a pH of 1, the dark reaction adsorption of RhB is inhibited by H+, but with the intervention of light, the degradation rate reaches 97.7% after nearly 5 min; this result was also reported in a study conducted by Bhaskar Bethi et al. [42]. It is speculated that the rapid degradation may be due to electron hole recombination occurring at high H+ concentrations [43]. Table 1 shows the photocatalytic degradation data obtained for RhB by the different catalysts, from which it is apparent that R4 has a good catalytic effect.

To expand the application of the catalysts, a 300-W Xe lamp was used for catalytic degradation of RhB (Fig. 6f). The CN/BiOBr heterojunction plays a crucial role in catalysis, and the introduction of carbon dots once again improved the photocatalytic performance of the composite material. RhB degradation rates of 99.0% and 98.5% were obtained by R4 and B1, respectively, after 20 min. However, testing of the photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline by R4 showed that 82.45% of the tetracycline could be degraded within 70 min under a 300-W Xe lamp. To further verify the actual performance of the material, the degradation of RhB by R4 under visible, outdoor light and comparison curves of the effects under different light sources are shown in Fig. 5(g-i). The experimental results indicate that R4 exhibits good catalytic degradation performance under all the light sources tested, which provides a theoretical basis for its practical application in the field (outdoor photocatalytic degradation of RhB can be observed in Fig. S14 and the video.S1
). The extended testing of composite materials reveals that they have desirable light absorption performance and catalytic universality.

Fig. 7 a is a sample diagram of the catalyst during and after the reaction, which clearly indicates the strong adsorption of RhB on the catalyst surface. In order to verify the cyclic catalytic performance of the material, R4 was used to catalyze the degradation of RhB, as shown in Fig. 7b. The degradation of RhB decreased from 99.54% to 98.87% after four rounds of catalysis, indicating desirable cyclic performance for the material. As shown in Fig. 7(c, d), no considerable changes were observed in the FTIR or XRD spectra of the catalyst before and after the reaction, indicating good stability for the material structure. Thus, adjusting the energy band structure with carbon dots is key to improve the cyclic performance of a catalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic mechanism and reaction path investigation

ce:section.ce:para: To further elucidate the mechanism for the photocatalytic degradation of RhB, ESR was used to detect the contribution of active species to the photocatalytic process. Fig. 8 (a, b) show the ESR spectra for the active radicals ·OH and ·O2 −, which are generated by the R4 catalyst during the reaction process. No free radicals were obtained in the dark; however, obvious signal peaks indicating the presence of ·OH and ·O2 − radicals were observed in the light, with the signal peak increasing over time. Free radical capture experiments were therefore conducted to verify the generation of active species during catalysis, with silver nitrate (AgNO3), isopropyl alcohol (IPA), AO, and 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ) selected as scavengers for electron (e−), hydroxyl radicals (·OH), photogenerated vacancies (h + ), and superoxide radicals (·O2 −), respectively. Fig. 8c shows the free radical capture curve for the R4 catalyst, The RhB degradation rate was reduced from 98.65% to 41.95% after the addition of AO and to 37.84% after adding BQ. These results indicate that h + and ·O2 − play a major role in the photocatalytic reaction. The radical experiments and ESR analysis demonstrate the key role that is played by the O2−, h+, and ·OH species in the photocatalytic oxidation of RhB.

The catalytic reaction mechanism was revealed by combining the energy band structure of the catalyst with free radical testing. As shown in Fig. 8d, the valence band of BiOBr was irradiated to generate photogenerated electrons, thus forming a hole that oxidized OH− to generate the hydroxyl radical, after which the photogenerated electrons migrate to the conduction band position. The carbon dot then transfers the electrons generated by BiOBr to the valence band of CN and finally, electrons from the CN valence band transition to the conduction band position, which generates superoxide radicals that degrade the RhB.

Based on the aforementioned results, two main factors contribute to the high photocatalytic activity of R4. On the one hand, the carbon quantum dot loading greatly improves the specific surface area of the material, resulting in a wealth of active sites and making the photocatalytic process more efficient. On the other hand, R4 forms an effective charge collection and separation system for a z-type heterostructure between CN and BiOBr, which forms a Schottky barrier, promotes electron separation and induces the generation of highly active radicals.

HPLC–MS is an important analytical method for studying the intermediates that are produced by degradation products during the reaction process [49]. Fig.S15 shows the UPLC–MS spectra of RhB during photocatalytic degradation at 0, 10, and 20 min. As shown in Fig.S15a, the RhB solution peaks at retention times of 9.52 and 9.80 min in the unreacted state. MS analysis indicates that this is mainly due to RhB dechlorination (m/z = 443) and the removal of one molecule of ethyl (m/z = 415). For catalytic degradation conducted over 10 min, Fig.S15b shows absorption peaks at 0.00–3.00 min and 7.70–10.00 min in the UPLC spectrum. Herein, we discuss the MS spectra obtained under a retention time of 7.70–10.00 min, for which the mass spectrum shows that the product (m/z = 415) sequentially removes ethyl, forming molecules with m/z = 387, m/z = 359, and m/z = 210 [50]. The compound (m/z = 210) then forms diphenyl ether (m/z = 170) by removing the cleavage from the two amino groups and the double bonds on both sides. Finally, we found that the retention time was mostly around 0.00–3.00 min if catalysis was allowed to continue for 20 min (Fig.S15c); however, there was still a m/z = 195 peak at the retention time of 7.70 min, indicating that the formation of diphenyl ether is the main catalytic step and that the phenol results from further diphenyl ether cleavage (m/z = 88). Meanwhile, the benzoic acid that peeled off from m/z = 359 forms small molecule compounds after hydrogenation (m/z = 126), hydroxylation (m/z = 144), bond breaking, carboxylation, and decarboxylation (m/z = 158) [51]. The final product then undergoes further degradation into CO2 and H2O. The reaction intermediate is shown in Fig. 9 .

The residual product HTC was not utilized when the CQDs were prepared. Hence, we used HTC with KOH to prepare active hydrothermal carbonization. Fig. 10 (a, b) are SEM images of the activated hydrothermal carbon. Its surface comprises a uniform pore structure, which is characterized by abundant numbers of pores and high density ad compared to traditional activated carbon. The drawback is that yields of only 7.1%, 6.5%, and 4.6% were obtained from HTC-1, HTC-2, and HTC-3, respectively, rendering their application unlikely. The surface functional group structure of the material was also characterized by infrared spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 10c. The activated carbon has a typical carbon material spectrum as compared to nonactivated carbon and the decrease in its peak from 1100 cm−1 to 1780 cm−1 indicates that aromatization occurs during the activation process [52]. To verify the adsorption performance of the material, we conducted a simple investigation of the adsorption performance of three types of hydrothermal-activated carbon, with the results shown in Fig. 10d. HCT-2 showed the best adsorption performance and is capable of adsorbing 95% a RhB solution in 10 min; we speculate that the low KOH dosage in this case led to a relatively small number of active sites, while excessive KOH makes the pore structure collapse, reducing the adsorption performance.

In summary, red and blue fluorescence-emission carbon quantum dots that were prepared using green tea as the source via different processes were used to promote the degradation of RhB in the Z heterojunctions of BiOBr/C3N4. The introduction of carbon quantum dots considerably improved the photoresponse and catalytic performance of the composite materials. Compared to blue light–emitting carbon dots, the sheet-like structure of red fluorescence-emitting carbon dots provides the catalyst with a higher specific surface area (89.26 m2 g−1). The higher number of active sites enabled R4 to degrade 98.48% of RhB within 15 min under UV light. In addition, the main active species of the catalyst are superoxide radicals and photogenerated holes. The hydrothermal-activated carbon derived from the remaining products after carbon dot preparation indicates that it can adsorb 95% of a RhB sample within 10 min. This study indicates that combining multicolor fluorescence emission carbon quantum dots with biomass and heterojunction catalysts provides a promising approach for the utilization and catalysis of waste biomass resources. In the future, new type of catalysts that use biomass carbon as catalyst carriers or band regulating materials will inevitably be one of the key directions for agriculture and forestry.
Yuan Zhong: Writing – original draft. Xinyu Zhang: Investigation. Yamei Wang: Writing – review & editing. Xiaotao Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Ximing Wang: Supervision.
